title: Advantages of the Various Methods
narrationScript: Every penetrant system on the qualified products list will find a flaw under the right conditions. The Level Two skill is choosing the system whose advantages match the job. Start with type. Fluorescent penetrant glows against a darkened booth, and your eye catches a faint glowing line far sooner than a faint red one, so Type One owns the high sensitivity work. Visible dye asks only for white light, which makes Type Two the choice for field and daylight inspection. Then choose the removal method. Water washable rinses off in one step, which buys speed, low cost, complex geometry, and rough castings. Postemulsifiable separates removal from the penetrant. The emulsifier or remover acts only on the surface excess, so shallow flaw entrapments survive washing. That control is why critical engine hardware runs hydrophilic Method D. Solvent removable trades line speed for portability. The whole examination travels in three aerosol cans. Finally, match the sensitivity level, one half through four, to the smallest flaw the requirement says you must find, not to the biggest number on the shelf.

sections.heading: Choosing the System Is Part of the Examination
sections.body:
A Level I runs the process as written. A Level II also helps decide *which* process to run — and that decision is made before the first drop of penetrant goes on. A drawing note that says "fluorescent penetrant inspect per ASTM E1417" still leaves real choices: which method of removal, and which sensitivity level.

The menu is fixed by classification. ASTM E1417 classifies penetrant systems by **Type** (I — fluorescent dye, II — visible dye), by **Method** of excess penetrant removal (A — water-washable, B — postemulsifiable lipophilic, C — solvent-removable, D — postemulsifiable hydrophilic), by developer **Form**, and by **Sensitivity Level** (½ through 4, fluorescent systems only). The materials themselves are qualified to **AMS 2644** and listed on the qualified products list, QPL-AMS-2644 — you select from qualified families, never assemble your own mix.

Selection is driven by a short list of questions. What is the smallest, tightest flaw the acceptance criteria require you to find? What is the surface — finish-machined, or rough as-cast? How many parts, and how big? Does the part come to a processing line, or must the examination travel to the part? This lesson covers the *advantages* side of that decision — what each type and method does better than the others. The disadvantages and failure modes come next lesson; selection takes both.

sections.caption: The selection menu per ASTM E1417 and AMS 2644: pick a type, pick a removal method, and — for fluorescent systems — pick a sensitivity level.

sections.heading: Fluorescent or Visible: What Each Type Buys
sections.body:
**Type I — fluorescent** owns the sensitivity advantage, and the reason is contrast physics. In a darkened booth under UV-A, the indication is a glowing yellow-green line on a nearly black field. The eye detects a far smaller quantity of fluorescent dye glowing in darkness than red dye sitting on a white background, so Type I reveals finer, tighter flaws — and it is the only type offered in graded sensitivity levels up to ultrahigh. That is why fluorescent penetrant is the default for aerospace acceptance work. Its price of admission is infrastructure: E1417 working numbers are at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A at the examination surface with ambient white light held to 2 footcandles or less — routine in a fixed booth, harder on a windy ramp.

**Type II — visible** buys freedom from that infrastructure. Indications are red against a white developer background and are read under ordinary white light — at least 100 footcandles at the surface, which a work light or daylight provides. No booth, no UV-A lamp, no dark adaptation. That makes visible dye the natural partner for field work: structural welds, tanks, piping, machinery repairs, one-off examinations far from a processing line.

One selection consequence belongs in this lesson: the choice of type is not freely reversible. Visible dye residues left in a flaw can **quench fluorescence**, so E1417 and most aerospace specifications prohibit fluorescent examination of a surface previously processed with visible penetrant. Choosing Type II today can foreclose a more sensitive re-examination tomorrow.

sections.heading: Method A — Water-Washable: Speed, Volume, Geometry
sections.body:
Method A penetrants carry their **emulsifier blended into the penetrant itself**, so the excess rinses away with a plain water spray in a single controlled step. Every advantage of the method flows from that one fact.

- **Throughput.** One removal step means the shortest process line and the fastest cycle — no separate emulsifier station, no timed emulsifier contact to supervise. For high-volume work it is the most economical method per part.
- **Geometry.** A water spray reaches what a wiping cloth never will: threads, splines, keyways, blind holes, internal passages, and deep recesses. Complex shapes process as easily as flat plate.
- **Rough surfaces.** As-cast and as-forged surfaces that would shred a wipe cloth and trap solvent-borne dye rinse clean under water. Water-washable systems are the classic choice for castings.
- **Scale, both directions.** Baskets of small parts dip and rinse as a batch; very large parts can be examined with the same materials because spray application and spray rinse scale up.

Typical Method A territory: production castings and forgings, large lots of machined parts, and any examination where part count or part size makes a one-step removal decisive. The advantage to remember for selection: **simplicity that scales** — the fewest process variables of any water-based method.

sections.heading: Methods B and D — Postemulsifiable: Removal You Can Control
sections.body:
Postemulsifiable penetrants take the emulsifier **out** of the penetrant. By itself the penetrant is not water-washable; it becomes removable only after an emulsifier (Method B) or remover (Method D) is applied as a separate, timed step. That separation is the whole advantage: washing acts on what the emulsification step prepared, not on the penetrant sitting inside a flaw — so shallow, broad entrapments survive removal that would strip a self-emulsifying penetrant. Control over removal is also what lets the highest-sensitivity examinations keep their faint indications.

**Method B — lipophilic.** An oil-based emulsifier, used **as supplied** with no dilution or concentration control, applied by immersion or flowing — never by spray, because it works by *diffusing into* the penetrant layer and contact time governs how deep that diffusion goes. Its advantages are simplicity of bath makeup, short contact times, and a long production history on high-volume fluorescent lines that need more sensitivity margin than Method A gives.

**Method D — hydrophilic.** A water-diluted, detergent-action remover that works by **displacement at the surface** — it scrubs excess penetrant off layer by layer and does not diffuse into the entrapment. A plain water pre-rinse ahead of the remover knocks down the bulk of the excess and extends bath life. It applies by immersion at the manufacturer's qualified concentration (checked with a refractometer) or by spray at low concentration — E1417 caps spray concentration at 5 percent. Method D offers the **most controllable removal in penetrant testing**, which is why fracture-critical aerospace hardware and Level 3 and 4 examinations are its home ground.

sections.caption: The postemulsifiable advantage: a self-emulsifying (Method A) penetrant is water-removable everywhere — including inside a shallow flaw — while a hydrophilic remover (Method D) acts only on the surface excess, so the entrapment survives to form an indication.

sections.heading: Method C — Solvent-Removable: Take the Examination to the Part
sections.body:
Method C inverts the logistics of every other method: instead of bringing parts to a processing line, the **examination travels to the part**. The working kit is three aerosol cans — cleaner/remover, penetrant, and nonaqueous developer — plus wiping cloths and a light meter. No water supply, no emulsifier station, no dryer (the solvent carriers flash off), and with Type II dye, no darkened area.

Its advantages decide real jobs:

- **Spot examination.** One weld repair, one bushing bore, one suspect area on a machined face — examined without processing, wetting, or re-protecting the entire article.
- **In-service work.** On-aircraft, on-bridge, inside plants and tank farms, at remote sites — wherever installed hardware cannot come to the shop.
- **Precision on smooth surfaces.** Removal is a dry wipe followed by a cloth *dampened* with solvent — flushing the surface with solvent is prohibited, because a solvent flood strips penetrant straight out of flaws. Done with care on a smooth machined surface, that gentle wipe removes the excess while barely disturbing the entrapment, which is why a carefully executed solvent-removable fluorescent examination of a small area can rival line sensitivity.
- **Same qualified chemistry.** Method C materials come from the same AMS 2644-qualified families as line systems, so field results stand under the same specifications.

The trade is speed: wipe removal is slow, deliberate handwork. Method C wins where portability and localized coverage matter more than throughput.

sections.caption: Method advantages at a glance — the selection matrix a Level II carries into planning. The governing procedure and engineering specification always control.
rows: Plain water spray; emulsifier is built into the penetrant
One-step removal; fastest and most economical; spray reaches complex geometry; handles rough as-cast surfaces; scales from baskets of small parts to very large parts
High-volume castings and forgings, production lots, large surface areas
Oil-based emulsifier used as supplied, applied by dip or flow for a timed contact, then water rinse
Removal under time control; no bath dilution or concentration checks; short contact times; better protection of shallow flaws than self-emulsifying penetrant
High-production fluorescent lines needing sensitivity margin beyond Method A
Dry wipe, then a lint-free cloth dampened with solvent (no flushing)
Entire examination from aerosol cans; no water, power, or fixed line; examines one local area without processing the whole part; careful wiping preserves entrapments on smooth surfaces
Field welds, in-service and on-aircraft spot examinations, remote sites
Water pre-rinse, detergent-action remover by immersion or low-concentration spray, final rinse
Most controllable removal — remover displaces surface excess only and does not diffuse into flaws; pre-rinse extends bath life; supports the highest-sensitivity examinations
Fracture-critical aerospace hardware, finish-machined parts, Level 3–4 work

sections.heading: Sensitivity Levels: Match the Level to the Requirement
sections.body:
Fluorescent penetrants are classified into five sensitivity levels under AMS 2644: **½ (ultralow), 1 (low), 2 (medium), 3 (high), and 4 (ultrahigh)**. A level is assigned during qualification by comparative testing against reference penetrant systems on cracked specimens, and it is a **fixed property of the listed product**. You cannot promote a Level 2 penetrant to Level 3 performance with longer dwell or a heavier coat — and Type II visible penetrants are not classified by level at all. When the requirement is fine, tight flaws found reliably, the answer is fluorescent.

Selection logic runs on three inputs:

- **The flaw you must find.** Tighter, shallower, finer flaws demand higher levels. Fracture-critical rotating engine hardware with low-cycle-fatigue cracks lives at Level 3 and 4; gross shrinkage in a rough casting does not need them.
- **The surface you must find it on.** Higher-sensitivity penetrants hold in surface roughness and produce fluorescent background. On an as-cast surface, a Level 4 penetrant can bury relevant indications in glow; a Level ½ or 1 keeps the field readable.
- **Process and cost.** Washability generally drops as sensitivity rises, removal control matters more, and the materials cost more per gallon.

A typical ladder — typical only, the engineering specification governs: **½–1** for rough castings and gross discontinuities; **2** as the workhorse for general machined aerospace hardware; **3** for critical finished parts; **4** for fracture-critical hardware and the tightest fatigue cracks. Two binding rules finish the job: a specified level is a floor — "Level 3 minimum" accepts Level 3 or 4, never Level 2 — and when the choice is yours, select the **lowest level that reliably reveals the flaws of concern**. Sensitivity beyond the requirement buys background, overcalls, and cost — not safety.

figures.title: Penetrant system classification per ASTM E1417 / AMS 2644
Why postemulsifiable removal protects shallow flaws

interactive.title: You Select the System
interactive.intro: You are the Level II planning penetrant examinations under a written procedure that allows any AMS 2644-qualified system meeting the engineering requirement. For each job, pick the system whose advantages fit — type, method, and level.

steps.prompt: Job 1: 400 aluminum pump housings per day, rough as-cast surfaces, processed in baskets on an automated line. The acceptance criteria target shrinkage porosity and cold shuts — relatively open, gross discontinuities.
options: Type I, Method D, Level 4 — maximum sensitivity is always the safest choice
Type I, Method A, Level 1 — water-washable at low sensitivity
Type II, Method C — aerosol spot checks on a sample from each basket
Type I, Method B, Level 3 — lipophilic postemulsifiable
steps.feedback: Rough castings in volume are Method A territory: one-step water removal scales to baskets, the spray rinses rough surfaces and complex passages clean, and the cost per part is the lowest available. The flaws of concern are gross, so a low sensitivity level finds them while keeping fluorescent background on the rough surface under control. Level 4 would drown the surface in glow; Method C cannot keep up with 400 parts a day and sampling is not 100 percent coverage.

steps.prompt: Job 2: a finish-machined titanium compressor disk — fracture-critical rotating hardware. The engineering specification requires reliable detection of tight low-cycle-fatigue cracks.
options: Type I, Method A, Level 2 — the general-purpose workhorse
Type II, Method C — red dye with careful wipe removal
Type I, Method D, Level 4 — hydrophilic postemulsifiable at ultrahigh sensitivity
Type I, Method A, Level ½ — lowest background possible
steps.feedback: Tight fatigue cracks on fracture-critical hardware call for ultrahigh sensitivity, and Type I is the only type offered in graded levels — visible dye is not classified by level and cannot match fluorescent contrast. Method D's hydrophilic remover acts only on surface excess, protecting the faint entrapments a Level 4 examination exists to find. The smooth machined surface keeps background manageable, so the high level costs little and buys the detection reliability the specification demands.

steps.prompt: Job 3: one weld repair on a carbon-steel storage tank in the field. No water supply, no electrical service at the work point, and the examination will be read in daylight.
options: Type II, Method C — visible dye aerosol kit with wipe removal
Type I, Method A — truck in a water tank and portable rinse station
Type I, Method D — the most controllable removal available
Defer the examination until the tank can be sectioned and shipped to the shop
steps.feedback: This is the textbook Method C job: the whole examination travels in aerosol cans, removal is a solvent-dampened wipe needing no water or power, and nonaqueous developer flash-dries without an oven. Type II visible dye reads under daylight — at least 100 footcandles at the surface — with no UV-A lamp or darkened area. Methods A and D are line processes that make no sense for one local weld on installed equipment, and tanks are not sectioned to suit an inspection method.

steps.prompt: Job 4: an engineering order for machined steel landing-gear components specifies "fluorescent penetrant, sensitivity Level 3 minimum." Your shop has a Level 2 system and a Level 4 system qualified and in tanks. What do you run?
options: The Level 2 system with doubled penetrant dwell to make up the difference
The Level 2 system — landing gear is not engine hardware, so Level 3 is overkill
Blend the Level 2 and Level 4 penetrants to approximate a Level 3
The Level 4 system — it meets or exceeds the specified minimum
steps.feedback: A sensitivity level is a fixed property assigned to the qualified product on QPL-AMS-2644 — no dwell time or technique change turns a Level 2 into a Level 3, and blending materials from different qualified families destroys the qualification of both. "Level 3 minimum" sets a floor: Level 4 satisfies it. Expect a little more background than a Level 3 would give on these machined surfaces, and proceed — the requirement, not your opinion of overkill, governs.

checkQuestions.question: Which advantage belongs specifically to Method A water-washable penetrant systems?
options: Excess penetrant is removed only after a separately applied emulsifier completes a timed contact
They offer the greatest resistance to overremoval of shallow, broad flaws
Excess penetrant rinses off with a plain water spray in a single step, because the emulsifier is built into the penetrant
They require no water at any point in the process
checkQuestions.explanation: Method A penetrants are self-emulsifying: the emulsifier is blended in, so removal is one controlled water rinse with no separate emulsification step. That single fact drives the method's speed, economy, and suitability for complex shapes and rough castings. The separately timed emulsifier describes Methods B and D, superior shallow-flaw protection belongs to the postemulsifiable methods, and the water-free process is Method C.

checkQuestions.question: Why does Method D (hydrophilic postemulsifiable) protect shallow, broad flaws better than Method A?
options: The penetrant itself is not water-washable, and the hydrophilic remover displaces only the surface excess — so the entrapment inside the flaw survives rinsing
The hydrophilic remover diffuses deep into the flaw and thickens the penetrant so it cannot wash out
Method D penetrants are far more viscous, which physically prevents them from leaving the flaw
The water pre-rinse seals the flaw mouth with a film of water before the remover is applied
checkQuestions.explanation: A postemulsifiable penetrant carries no built-in emulsifier, so water alone cannot strip it from a flaw. The hydrophilic remover works by detergent displacement at the surface, layer by layer, and does not diffuse into the entrapment — removal stops where the excess ends. Diffusion into the penetrant describes the lipophilic (Method B) mechanism, not a flaw-sealing effect, and neither viscosity nor the pre-rinse is what preserves the entrapment.

checkQuestions.question: A Level II must examine one weld repair on installed plant equipment with no water supply or processing line available. Which method advantage decides the selection?
options: Method A — a built-in emulsifier means the weld can be rinsed with any available liquid
Method B — the lipophilic emulsifier needs no concentration control in the field
Method D — its spray application works at concentrations of 5 percent or less
Method C — the complete examination travels in aerosol cans, with excess removed by a solvent-dampened wipe
checkQuestions.explanation: Method C is built for exactly this job: cleaner/remover, penetrant, and nonaqueous developer all come in aerosol cans, removal is a wipe rather than a rinse, and the developer flash-dries without an oven — no water, power, or fixed line required. Methods A, B, and D are all water-rinse processes tied to processing facilities, whatever their other advantages.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about penetrant sensitivity levels under ASTM E1417 and AMS 2644 is correct?
options: Levels ½ through 4 apply equally to fluorescent and visible dye penetrants
Levels run from ½ (ultralow) to 4 (ultrahigh), apply to Type I fluorescent systems only, and are fixed properties of the qualified product
A penetrant's level can be raised one step by extending dwell time and applying a heavier developer coat
Level 4 is mandatory for all aerospace penetrant examinations
checkQuestions.explanation: AMS 2644 assigns the five levels — ½, 1, 2, 3, 4 — to fluorescent penetrants during qualification testing, and the level belongs to the listed product itself. Visible Type II penetrants are not classified by level, no technique change alters a product's assigned level, and specifications call out the level each part actually requires — Level 4 is reserved for the most critical work, not mandated everywhere.

checkQuestions.question: Why is the highest available sensitivity level NOT the default choice for rough as-cast surfaces?
options: High-sensitivity penetrants are too thin to enter large discontinuities
ASTM E1417 prohibits Level 3 and Level 4 penetrants on castings
Higher-sensitivity penetrants hold in surface roughness and produce fluorescent background that can mask relevant indications — while costing more and washing less easily
Rough surfaces can only be examined with visible dye penetrants
checkQuestions.explanation: Sensitivity is matched to the requirement, not maximized. On rough surfaces a high-level penetrant clings in the texture, and the resulting background glow can hide the indications that matter; washability also drops and cost rises as level climbs. E1417 imposes no casting prohibition, capillary entry is not the issue, and rough castings are routinely examined with low-level fluorescent systems.

references: ASTM E1417/E1417M, Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Testing (system classification, removal methods, lighting and remover-concentration limits)
SAE AMS 2644, Inspection Material, Penetrant (material qualification, sensitivity levels ½–4, QPL-AMS-2644)
ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024), PT Level II Topical Outline §2.1 (advantages of the various methods)
ASTM E165/E165M, Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Examination for General Industry (method guidance and technique context)
